past_medical_history: Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 12 years ago, with peripheral neuropathy
Eosinophilic vasculitis, recently diagnosed 3 months ago, treated with methotrexate (now discontinued)
Prior retinal detachment in the left eye, surgically repaired 4 years ago

medications.indication: Initiated at outside facility for suspected seizures (no confirmed efficacy)

family_history: Mother had Parkinson's disease, diagnosed at age 72
Father had type 2 diabetes and coronary artery disease
No family history of epilepsy or autoimmune disease

social_history.tobacco: Former smoker, quit 10 years ago, 15 pack-year history
social_history.living_situation: Lives alone following the death of his wife 18 months ago. Primary caregiver for his elderly mother who has dementia.
social_history.recent_stressors: Significant stress related to recent eosinophilic vasculitis diagnosis. Anxiety about COVID-19 vaccination and its potential interaction with his immunosuppressive history. Grief from bereavement and caregiver burden.
social_history.exercise: Previously walked daily, now largely bedbound due to fear of triggering episodes

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert, oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Glasgow Coma Scale 15. Speech fluent with normal rate and rhythm. No dysarthria or aphasia. Attention intact, serial sevens performed without error. Recall 3/3 objects at 5 minutes. Affect mildly anxious with visible distress when discussing his illness. No thought disorder. Cooperative with examination.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II: Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally. Pupils equal, round, reactive to light 3mm to 2mm bilaterally. Prior left retinal surgery noted but fundoscopic exam otherwise unremarkable, no papilledema. CN III/IV/VI: Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus or diplopia. CN V: Sensation intact to light touch in all three divisions bilaterally. Masseter strength symmetric. CN VII: Face symmetric at rest and with activation, no facial weakness. CN VIII: Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. CN IX/X: Palate elevates symmetrically, gag reflex intact. CN XI: Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. CN XII: Tongue midline, no fasciculations or atrophy.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal throughout. Tone normal in all extremities, no spasticity, rigidity, or cogwheel phenomenon. Upper extremities: inconsistent give-way weakness on bilateral elbow flexion, initially generating 4+/5 force then suddenly collapsing — this pattern is not consistent with organic weakness. Lower extremities: give-way weakness on bilateral hip flexion and knee extension, more pronounced on the left. Strength is full (5/5) when tested with distraction techniques. No pronator drift. No muscle atrophy or fasciculations.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact in the upper extremities. Decreased vibration sense at the toes bilaterally consistent with known diabetic peripheral neuropathy. Proprioception intact at the great toes. No dermatomal or lateralized sensory loss beyond the expected stocking-pattern neuropathy.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally. Patellar 2+ bilaterally. Achilles 1+ bilaterally (diminished, consistent with diabetic neuropathy). Plantar responses flexor (downgoing) bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing normal bilaterally without dysmetria or intention tremor. Heel-to-shin testing normal bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements intact.
neurological_exam.gait: Patient was reluctant to ambulate, stating he might trigger an episode. When assisted to standing, gait was cautious but no focal abnormalities observed. No ataxia, no steppage gait, no Trendelenburg sign. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: During the examination, the patient had a witnessed episode lasting approximately 45 seconds. He first reported a pulsating sensation in his ears, followed by facial grimacing and bilateral lower extremity tremor that progressed to violent shaking of all four limbs. The shaking was arrhythmic, waxing and waning. His eyes were open but he appeared unresponsive. Vitals during the event were: HR 78, BP 132/82, SpO2 98%. After the shaking stopped, the patient lay still with eyes closed for approximately 2 minutes, during which he later reported he was fully aware but unable to move or speak. He denied loss of consciousness, tongue biting, and incontinence. Peripheral cyanosis of the toes was observed transiently. The episode self-resolved. Continuous EEG monitoring was in place during this event.

patient.chief_complaint: Seizure-like limb movements and episodes of unresponsiveness for one month
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Gerald Novak is a 64-year-old right-handed Caucasian man who was transferred from an outside hospital to the neurology inpatient service for evaluation of recurrent seizure-like episodes over the past month. He was initially admitted to the outside facility after his primary care physician observed an episode of violent limb shaking during an office visit. At the outside hospital, levetiracetam was started empirically for suspected seizures and low-dose diazepam was administered during acute episodes, neither of which provided any benefit. He was also receiving methotrexate for a recently diagnosed eosinophilic vasculitis, which was discontinued prior to transfer due to concern for a possible CNS inflammatory process. The episodes are described as beginning with a pulsating sensation in both ears, followed by a wave of tension and pain radiating down his arms and legs, facial grimacing, and tremors of the lower extremities that rapidly escalate into violent bilateral shaking. He reports retaining full awareness throughout the shaking phase and can hear and understand those around him. He consistently denies loss of consciousness, tongue biting, or urinary incontinence. Each shaking event lasts between 30 seconds and 2 minutes and is followed by a period of approximately 2 minutes during which he lies motionless, appears unresponsive, but later reports complete awareness with an inability to move or speak. Transient peripheral cyanosis of the toes has been noted after some episodes. The events occur multiple times per day, predominantly in the presence of medical staff or visitors. His oxygen saturation, heart rate, and blood pressure have remained stable during and after each observed event. He reports palpitations coinciding with some episodes. His medical background is significant for type 2 diabetes with known peripheral neuropathy, hypertension, prior retinal detachment, and the recent eosinophilic vasculitis diagnosis. He lives alone following the death of his wife 18 months ago and is the primary caregiver for his elderly mother. He reports substantial psychological distress regarding his vasculitis diagnosis and ongoing anxiety about COVID-19 vaccination given his prior immunosuppressive therapy. He has become increasingly fearful of activity, spending most of his day in bed to avoid triggering episodes.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9.5 Hz symmetric alpha rhythm, well-modulated and reactive to eye opening
background.sleep_architecture: Normal sleep transients observed during drowsiness including vertex waves and K-complexes

artifacts.note: Present during the clinical event; underlying cerebral rhythms were identifiable and normal

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Performed for 3 minutes, produced appropriate symmetric slowing, no abnormal discharges, did not provoke a clinical event
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving response at 10-20 Hz, no photoparoxysmal response

eeg.impression: NORMAL EEG. Continuous video-EEG monitoring captured a typical clinical event with bilateral limb shaking. The EEG remained entirely normal during the episode with no ictal or post-ictal electrographic correlate. No interictal epileptiform discharges were identified. Background activity is normal and symmetric for age. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Monitoring period was 48 hours; three clinical events were captured, all with the same electroclinical dissociation pattern.

signal_characteristics.T1: Normal gray-white matter differentiation. Age-appropriate cortical volume.
signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: A few scattered punctate white matter hyperintensities in the periventricular and deep white matter, consistent with chronic microvascular ischemic changes in the setting of hypertension and diabetes. No confluent lesions. No juxtacortical or infratentorial lesions to suggest demyelination.
signal_characteristics.DWI: No restricted diffusion to suggest acute ischemia
signal_characteristics.Post_contrast: No abnormal parenchymal or meningeal enhancement. No suggestion of vasculitis-related enhancement.

additional_observations: Ventricles are normal in size and configuration for age
Basal ganglia, thalami, and brainstem are normal in signal and morphology
Major intracranial vessels demonstrate normal flow voids
No focal edema or vasculitis-related changes
mri.impression: No evidence of cerebral vasculitis, acute infarction, demyelination, encephalitis, or mass lesion. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings: No left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria
ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm at 74 bpm. No acute ischemic changes. No arrhythmia. Normal intervals.

Epilepsy_Markers.test: Prolactin (post-event, drawn 25 min after captured episode)

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9.5 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric and reactive

artifacts.note: Present during events but underlying EEG remained interpretable

output.impression: NORMAL prolonged video-EEG. Seven habitual clinical events were captured, all without any EEG correlate. No epileptiform discharges identified at any time during the 72-hour recording. Clinical correlation recommended.

special_tests.CSF PCR panel: Negative — HSV-1, HSV-2, VZV, enterovirus, CMV, HHV-6, parechovirus DNA not detected

output.interpretation: Opening pressure: 14 cmH2O. WBC: 2 cells/uL (normal: 0-5) (N/A PMN/N/A lymph). Protein: 38 mg/dL (normal: 15-45). Glucose: 72 mg/dL (normal: 40-70) (ratio 0.44 (serum glucose 162 mg/dL)). Oligoclonal bands: Negative (no CSF-specific bands). IgG index: 0.52 (normal: <0.7). CSF PCR panel: Negative — HSV-1, HSV-2, VZV, enterovirus, CMV, HHV-6, parechovirus DNA not detected. VDRL: Non-reactive. Cytology: No malignant cells. Anti-NMDA receptor antibodies: Negative. Anti-LGI1 antibodies: Negative. Anti-CASPR2 antibodies: Negative.

signal_characteristics.T2: Mild disc desiccation at C5-C6 and L4-L5. No disc herniation or central canal stenosis. No cord signal abnormality.

additional_observations: Spinal cord is normal in caliber and signal intensity throughout
No intramedullary or intradural extramedullary lesions
Conus medullaris terminates at L1 level, normal
output.impression: MRI of the cervical and lumbar spine shows only mild age-appropriate degenerative disc changes. No spinal cord lesions, no myelopathy, no demyelinating plaques, no structural cause for the patient's symptoms. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.query: psychogenic non-epileptic seizures functional neurological disorder diagnosis management

results.title: Psychogenic nonepileptic seizures: A review
results.summary: PNES account for 5-20% of patients referred for refractory epilepsy. Video-EEG is the gold standard for diagnosis. Key features include preserved awareness, asynchronous limb movements, side-to-side head movements, forced eye closure, and absence of post-ictal confusion. Normal post-event prolactin supports the diagnosis.

results.title: Functional neurological disorder: new subtypes and shared mechanisms
results.authors: Espay AJ, Aybek S, Carson A, et al.
results.summary: FND is a diagnosis made by positive clinical signs (e.g., Hoover sign, inconsistent weakness, distractibility) rather than solely by exclusion. Updated DSM-5 criteria require demonstration of internal inconsistency. Treatment involves multidisciplinary approach: neuropsychiatry, specialized physiotherapy, and CBT.

results.title: Delivering the diagnosis of functional neurological disorder
results.summary: How the diagnosis is communicated significantly impacts prognosis. Explanation should include: the diagnosis name, that it is common and real, that the nervous system is not damaged, and that treatment is available. Avoid framing as 'diagnosis of exclusion' or implying symptoms are feigned.

output.summary: Video-EEG is the gold standard for diagnosing PNES, demonstrating normal cerebral activity during clinical events. FND is diagnosed by positive clinical signs (inconsistency, distractibility, Hoover sign), not merely by exclusion. Multidisciplinary management including neuropsychiatry, specialized physiotherapy, and CBT is the recommended treatment approach. Prognosis depends heavily on how the diagnosis is communicated.

interactions: No significant pharmacokinetic interactions with metformin, lisinopril, or gabapentin
warnings: Levetiracetam is not indicated in this patient given confirmed PNES/FND diagnosis — no epileptic seizures identified
Potential psychiatric adverse effects include irritability, behavioral changes, depression, and anxiety — particularly concerning in a patient with ongoing psychosocial stressors
Discontinuation should be gradual to avoid withdrawal symptoms, though seizure risk from discontinuation is not a concern as the patient does not have epilepsy
alternatives: No anti-seizure medication is indicated — recommend tapering and discontinuation
For PNES/FND: consider CBT, psychotherapy, specialized physiotherapy as primary treatments

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Functional neurological disorder with psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES)

differential.key_distinguishing: Normal EEG during captured clinical events rules out epileptic seizures; preserved awareness during episodes, normal post-event prolactin, and lack of response to levetiracetam all argue against epilepsy
Normal brain MRI with no focal edema, enhancement, or vasculitic changes; normal inflammatory markers; eosinophilia resolved
Negative anti-NMDAR, LGI1, and CASPR2 antibodies in both serum and CSF; normal CSF cell count and protein; no MRI signal changes in limbic structures
Normal CSF with no pleocytosis; negative comprehensive PCR panel; afebrile; no meningeal signs

differential.diagnosis: Progressive encephalomyelitis with rigidity and myoclonus (PERM) / Stiff-person spectrum
differential.key_distinguishing: Normal EMG with no continuous motor unit activity; negative anti-GAD65 and anti-amphiphysin antibodies; episodes are not consistent with startle-induced myoclonus

optimal_actions.action: Obtain continuous video-EEG monitoring to capture habitual clinical events
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal EEG during clinical seizure-like events, confirming PNES

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with contrast to evaluate for structural lesions and vasculitis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal brain MRI, no vasculitis or structural lesion

optimal_actions.action: Obtain comprehensive laboratory panel including CBC, BMP, TSH, inflammatory markers, and post-event prolactin
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal labs except known diabetes; normal post-event prolactin supporting non-epileptic etiology

optimal_actions.action: Perform EMG/NCS to evaluate for myopathy and peripheral nerve disease
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No myopathy; mild sensory neuropathy consistent with diabetes only

optimal_actions.action: Obtain autoimmune encephalitis antibody panel to rule out autoimmune etiology
Communicate diagnosis of PNES/FND supportively to patient, emphasizing that the condition is real, common, and treatable
Refer to psychiatry for evaluation of comorbid anxiety and depression, and initiation of psychotherapy (CBT)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Identification of adjustment disorder with significant anxiety and depression as contributing factors

optimal_actions.action: Taper and discontinue levetiracetam as there is no indication for anti-seizure medication
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Confirmation that levetiracetam is not indicated and may contribute to psychiatric side effects

critical_actions: Perform video-EEG monitoring during a habitual clinical event to establish the diagnosis of PNES
Obtain post-event prolactin to differentiate from epileptic seizures
Recognize positive functional signs on neurological exam (give-way weakness, inconsistency with distraction)
Communicate FND/PNES diagnosis in a supportive, non-dismissive manner
Refer for psychiatric and psychological evaluation and treatment
contraindicated_actions: Starting or escalating anti-seizure medications when EEG shows no epileptic activity
Administering benzodiazepines as acute treatment for PNES episodes
Ordering invasive procedures (e.g., brain biopsy) for suspected vasculitis without imaging evidence
Dismissing symptoms as 'not real' or implying malingering
Initiating immunosuppressive therapy for suspected CNS vasculitis without supportive evidence
key_reasoning_points: The patient's seizure-like episodes lack EEG correlates during video monitoring, which is the gold standard for diagnosing PNES
Preserved awareness during convulsive episodes, absence of post-ictal confusion, and normal post-event prolactin all support a non-epileptic etiology
Give-way weakness on examination that resolves with distraction is a positive functional sign, supporting FND diagnosis
Episodes occurring predominantly in the presence of staff, stable vitals throughout, and lack of response to anti-seizure medications are additional clinical clues
The differential including cerebral vasculitis is appropriately considered given the history of eosinophilic vasculitis, but normal MRI and inflammatory markers effectively exclude CNS involvement
Autoimmune encephalitis is appropriately excluded by negative antibody panels in both serum and CSF
PERM/stiff-person spectrum is excluded by normal EMG without continuous motor unit activity and negative anti-GAD65 antibodies
Multiple psychosocial stressors (bereavement, caregiver burden, health anxiety) provide a clear context for functional symptoms
FND is a diagnosis made by positive clinical signs and electroclinical dissociation, not merely by exclusion of organic disease

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward case with clear electroclinical dissociation on video-EEG, positive functional signs on exam (give-way weakness), identifiable psychosocial stressors, and a systematic workup that excludes organic etiologies. The clinical features strongly point toward PNES/FND with minimal diagnostic ambiguity.
